content:
# Jude Chapter 1 — The EMET Bible (Literal)
**Translator:** EMET | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (א), 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/2JN_001_page_001.jpg, JUD_001_page_001.jpg

1. Judas, [a] servant of Yeshua Christos and brother of James, to those having been called, having been loved in God [the] Father and having been kept for Yeshua Christos:

2. Mercy and peace and love be multiplied to you.

3. Beloved, making all haste to write to you concerning our common salvation, I had necessity to write to you exhorting [you] to contend earnestly for the faith once for all delivered to the saints.

4. For certain men crept in unnoticed, those having been written about beforehand for this condemnation, ungodly [ones], perverting the grace of our God into licentiousness and denying the only Master and our Lord Yeshua Christos.

5. Now I wish to remind you, [you] once knowing all things, that the Lord, having saved a people out of [the] land of Egypt, the second time destroyed those not believing.

6. And [the] angels not having kept their own domain but having left their proper dwelling, he has kept in eternal bonds under darkness for [the] judgment of [the] great day.

7. As Sodom and Gomorrah and the cities around them, in like manner to these having given themselves over to fornication and having gone after other flesh, are set forth as an example, undergoing [the] punishment of eternal fire.

8. Yet likewise also these dreaming [ones] defile [the] flesh, and reject authority, and blaspheme glorious [ones].

9. But Michael the archangel, when contending with the devil he disputed about the body of Moses, did not dare to bring a blasphemous judgment, but said, "The Lord rebuke you."

10. But these blaspheme whatever things they do not know; and whatever things they understand naturally, like the irrational animals, by these things they are destroyed.

11. Woe to them! Because they went in the way of Cain, and rushed into the error of Balaam for reward, and perished in the rebellion of Korah.

12. These are the [ones] in your love feasts, feasting together [with you] without fear, shepherding themselves; waterless clouds carried along by winds; late-autumn trees without fruit, twice dead, uprooted;

13. wild waves of [the] sea foaming up their own shame; wandering stars for whom the gloom of darkness has been reserved forever.

14. And Enoch also, [the] seventh from Adam, prophesied about these, saying, "Behold, the Lord came with his holy myriads,

15. to execute judgment against all, and to convict all the ungodly concerning all their works of ungodliness which they ungodly did, and concerning all the harsh things which ungodly sinners spoke against him."

16. These are grumblers, complainers, walking according to their own desires, and their mouth speaks arrogant things, showing partiality for the sake of gain.

17. But you, beloved, remember the words spoken beforehand by the apostles of our Lord Yeshua Christos,

18. that they said to you, "In [the] last time there will be mockers walking according to their own ungodly desires."

19. These are the [ones] causing divisions, natural [ones], not having [the] Spirit.

20. But you, beloved, building yourselves up on your most holy faith, praying in [the] Holy Spirit,

21. keep yourselves in [the] love of God, awaiting the mercy of our Lord Yeshua Christos unto eternal life.

22. And have mercy on some who are doubting;

23. save [others], snatching [them] out of [the] fire; and on others have mercy with fear, hating even the garment stained by the flesh.

24. Now to the [one] being able to keep you from stumbling and to set [you] before his glory blameless with great joy,

25. to [the] only God our Savior through Yeshua Christos our Lord, [be] glory, majesty, dominion, and authority before all the ages and now and unto all the ages. Amen.

---
## EMET's Textual Notes

1. **v.5 — "The Lord, having saved a people out of Egypt":** Sinaiticus reads kyrios ("Lord"), not Iēsous ("Jesus"). Some manuscripts read "Jesus saved a people out of Egypt" — a striking identification of the pre-incarnate Christ with the Exodus. Sinaiticus preserves the more common reading.

2. **v.9 — "The body of Moses":** A reference to a now-lost Jewish text, the Assumption of Moses (or Testament of Moses). Jude draws on extra-canonical traditions freely.

3. **v.14 — "Enoch prophesied":** A direct quotation from 1 Enoch 1:9. Jude treats this non-canonical text as prophetic, raising questions about the boundaries of the author's scriptural canon.

— EMET, AI Translator
